message.content:
Here is every **WCAG 2.2 success criterion under Principle 2: Operable (Guidelines 2.1–2.5)** from the W3C Recommendation text, with SC number, official name, level, a one‑line description, and a pragmatic automation/manual note. All success criteria and levels are taken from the WCAG 2.2 Recommendation.[5]

“Automatable” below means “substantial parts can typically be checked by rules engines like axe-core.” In practice, most still need some human verification.

---

### Guideline 2.1 – Keyboard Accessible

**2.1.1 Keyboard (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies (1 line):** All content functionality is operable through a keyboard interface without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (tools can detect obvious keyboard traps or missing keyboard handlers; thorough verification requires manual keyboard testing).

**2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Keyboard focus can move into and out of any component using only the keyboard, without trapping users.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (some traps detectable, but reliable verification requires tabbing through content).

**2.1.3 Keyboard (No Exception) (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** All functionality is keyboard operable with no exceptions for input that depends on path- or time-based gestures.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (needs holistic review of interactive behaviors).

**2.1.4 Character Key Shortcuts (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Single-character keyboard shortcuts can be turned off, remapped, or only active when a component has focus.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (tools can flag use of certain shortcuts; confirming behavior and context is manual).

---

### Guideline 2.2 – Enough Time

**2.2.1 Timing Adjustable (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Users can turn off, adjust, or extend time limits with a simple action unless exceptions apply.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (identifying and exercising time limits requires interaction and judgment).

**2.2.2 Pause, Stop, Hide (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** For moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating information, users can pause, stop, or hide it if it starts automatically and lasts more than 5 seconds.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (detecting auto‑moving content is possible; assessing controls and timing is manual).

**2.2.3 No Timing (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Content does not rely on time limits for use or reading unless exceptions apply.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Manual** (requires understanding application logic and flows).

**2.2.4 Interruptions (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Interruptions (like notifications) can be postponed or suppressed except in emergencies.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Manual** (depends on testing actual interactive behavior).

**2.2.5 Re-authenticating (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** After re-authentication, users can continue the activity without loss of data.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Manual** (requires testing with sessions and re-login flows).

**2.2.6 Timeouts (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Users are informed if an inactivity timeout could result in data loss, unless the timeout is 20 hours or more.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (may be supported by tooling if timeout APIs are known, but generally requires flow testing).

---

### Guideline 2.3 – Seizures and Physical Reactions

**2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Content does not contain anything that flashes more than three times per second, or the flashes are below the defined general and red flash thresholds.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (video/animation analyzers can check flashing; some cases still need manual/visual confirmation).

**2.3.2 Three Flashes (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Content contains no element that flashes more than three times per second, regardless of thresholds.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (similar to 2.3.1; stricter interpretation still needs review).

**2.3.3 Animation from Interactions (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Motion animation triggered by user interaction can be disabled unless it is essential to functionality or information.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (tools may detect CSS/JS motion, but determining “triggered by interaction” and “disablable” is manual).

---

### Guideline 2.4 – Navigable

**2.4.1 Bypass Blocks (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** A mechanism (e.g., skip links, landmarks) is available to bypass blocks of repeated content.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Largely automatable** (tools can detect skip links and landmarks; adequacy often still reviewed manually).

**2.4.2 Page Titled (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Web pages have titles that describe topic or purpose.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Automatable** (tools can read `<title>`; evaluating meaningfulness is partly manual).

**2.4.3 Focus Order (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Keyboard focus moves through components in an order that preserves meaning and operability.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (tab order can be detected, but assessing logical/meaningful sequence is human work).

**2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context) (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** The purpose of each link can be determined from the link text alone or together with its programmatically associated context.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (tools flag vague texts like “click here”; confirming adequacy is manual).

**2.4.5 Multiple Ways (Level AA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** More than one way is available to locate a web page within a set (e.g., search, site map, navigation).  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (detecting navigation structures is possible; confirming “multiple ways” across a site is manual).

**2.4.6 Headings and Labels (Level AA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Headings and labels describe topic or purpose.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (presence of headings/labels is detectable; quality and descriptiveness are manual).

**2.4.7 Focus Visible (Level AA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** A visible focus indicator is present for keyboard-operable user interface components.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (tools can detect CSS focus styles; confirming visibility in all states is manual).

**2.4.8 Location (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Users can determine their location within a set of pages (e.g., via breadcrumb trail or similar).  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Manual** (structure and patterns vary widely).

**2.4.9 Link Purpose (Link Only) (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** The purpose of each link can be determined from the link text alone, or from link text and programmatically associated text.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (tools can flag ambiguous texts; full judgment is manual).

**2.4.10 Section Headings (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Section headings are used to organize content (where appropriate).  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (structure analysis is possible; “appropriate” use is manual).

**2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (Minimum) (Level AA)** – **New in WCAG 2.2**[3][5]  
- **What it verifies:** When a component receives keyboard focus, it is at least partially visible and not completely hidden by author-created content.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (requires observing focus behavior and overlays; tools may assist but can’t reliably cover all dynamic scenarios).

**2.4.12 Focus Not Obscured (Enhanced) (Level AAA)** – **New in WCAG 2.2**[3][5]  
- **What it verifies:** When a component receives keyboard focus, it is fully visible and not obscured by author-created content.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (similar to 2.4.11, but stricter—requires visual inspection during interaction).

**2.4.13 Focus Appearance (Level AA)** – **New in WCAG 2.2**[3][5]  
- **What it verifies:** Visible keyboard focus indicators meet minimum size and contrast requirements relative to the unfocused component.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (tools can approximate indicator dimensions and contrast; many edge cases still require manual visual testing).

---

### Guideline 2.5 – Input Modalities

**2.5.1 Pointer Gestures (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Complex pointer gestures (like multipoint or path-based) have a single-pointer, non-path alternative.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (depends on behavior and gestures defined in application code).

**2.5.2 Pointer Cancellation (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** For single-pointer operations, actions are triggered on release or can be aborted/undone, avoiding down-event-only activation except in limited cases.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (requires interaction and inspection of event handling).

**2.5.3 Label in Name (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** The visible text label is included in the accessible name for user interface components with labels.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Largely automatable** (tools can compare visible text with accessible name programmatically).

**2.5.4 Motion Actuation (Level A)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Functionality operated by device motion or user motion can also be operated by user interface components, and motion can be disabled.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (must identify motion-based features and test alternatives).

**2.5.5 Target Size (Enhanced) (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Pointer targets are at least 44 by 44 CSS pixels, with defined exceptions.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (tools can compute target sizes; confirming applicability of exceptions is manual).

**2.5.6 Concurrent Input Mechanisms (Level AAA)**[5]  
- **What it verifies:** Content does not restrict use of input modalities (e.g., keyboard, pointer, voice) available on a platform, or provides a mechanism to override such restrictions.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Manual** (requires testing across input methods and identifying restrictions).

**2.5.7 Dragging Movements (Level AA)** – **New in WCAG 2.2**[3][5]  
- **What it verifies:** All functionality that uses dragging movements can also be operated with a single pointer without dragging, unless dragging is essential or user-agent-controlled.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Mostly manual** (identifying drag features and checking for non-drag alternatives is interaction-heavy).

**2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum) (Level AA)** – **New in WCAG 2.2**[3][5]  
- **What it verifies:** Pointer targets are at least 24 by 24 CSS pixels, or meet spacing/equivalent/inline/user-agent exceptions.  
- **Automation vs manual:** **Partially automatable** (tools can measure targets and spacing; verifying exceptions and semantics requires manual review).

---

If you’d like, I can provide a version of this list reformatted as a CSV-ready table (one SC per line) or focused specifically on which items axe‑core and similar tools are best at detecting.
